---
Name: Mady Boyd, Emmanuella Obidike, Chloe Washington, Sidney Cerami, Joshua Hall
title: "R Notebook"
output: html_notebook
---

Access to healthcare is not the same for everyone. Differences often depend on income, where people live, and demographic factors. Although healthcare data is widely available, it can be hard to understand without proper analysis and visualization. Because of this difficulty, people are not able to clearly see where the gaps and differences in access to healthcare exist and what factors could be the potential causes for those gaps. The purpose of this project is to use a form of data analysis and visualization to break down and understand patterns regarding healthcare access and point out those disparities across different demographics and populations. With this project, complex healthcare data will be easier to interpret and understand for people like students, researchers, and all.

This project aims to explore healthcare access disparities through a data-focused and visualization-centered approach using the R programming language. By analyzing measurable factors such as income, insurance coverage, geographic location, and demographic characteristics, the project seeks to make complex healthcare data more accessible and understandable to students, researchers, and other stakeholders. 31b8e172-b470-440e-83d8-e6b185028602::31b8e172-b470-440e-83d8-e6b185028602

# Project Overview

This document represents my individual contribution to the project "Exploring Healthcare Access Disparities Through Data-Driven Visualization and Analysis." My primary responsibility was the Data Engineering and Processing of the 2021 baseline.

# Madison Boyd Individual Contribution

Role: Data Cleaning, Structural Engineering, and Processing Logic (2021).

My work focused on the complex deconstruction of six raw data files to create an efficient master dataset. This involved designing a modular cleaning pipeline, resolving schema inconsistencies, and executing a rigorous data type audit to ensure all socioeconomic and health variables were mathematically valid for analysis. All technical logic and processing steps presented here reflect my individual work in establishing the project's foundational dataset.

# Data Cleaning and Processing Logic (2021)

The 2021 analytical baseline was built using a modular cleaning pipeline. Since the County Health Rankings data is stored in specialized files, the method required a "Deconstruction and Extraction" approach to separate the metrics that are important for the project.

# Ingestion of Socioeconomic Predictors (Files 1 & 2)

The first step involved processing the "Additional Measure Data" along with the metadata for this file. The main challenge was the presence of metadata headers in the raw CSV. This required using the skip = 1 argument to match the headers with the data rows.

```{r clean_2021_file1}
#Processing File 1: Additional Measure Data 
# Standardizing 'null' values to handle suppressed data points

 clean21_measures <- read.csv("data/raw/2021/Additional Measure Data.csv", skip = 1, na.strings = c("N/A", "", " ")) %>% dplyr::select(FIPS, State, County, Median.Household.Income, X..Uninsured, X..Rural) %>% filter(County != "") 

# Verification: Ensure Socioeconomic columns are present names(clean21_measures) 
```

# Normalizing Health Rankings (Files 3 & 4)

Files 3 (Outcomes and Factors Rankings) and 4 (Outcomes and Factors SubRankings) gave the "calculated results" of the study. Because the raw files used generic headers like Rank and Rank.1, I used a Descriptive Renaming Strategy during selection to avoid variable collision when creating the final master join.

```{r clean_2021_file3_4}
# Processing File 3: Core Rankings 
clean21_ranked <- read.csv("data/raw/2021/Outcomes and Factors Rankings.csv", skip = 1) %>% dplyr::select(FIPS, Health_Outcomes_Rank = Rank, Health_Factors_Rank = Rank.1) 

# Processing File 4: Specialized Sub-Rankings 

clean21_subranked <- read.csv("data/raw/2021/Outcomes and Factors SubRankings.csv", skip = 1) %>% dplyr::select(FIPS, Length_of_Life_Rank = Rank, Clinical_Care_Rank = Rank.3, Socioeconomic_Rank = Rank.4)
```

# Isolating Racial Disparity Metrics (Files 5 & 6)

File 5 (Ranked Measure Data) had over 100 columns, including raw rates, confidence intervals, and data reliability flags. To keep the focus on racial equity, I used the contains() function to target the main Years of Potential Life Lost (YPLL) rates for Black and White populations while discarding secondary statistics.

```{r clean_2021_file5}
 # Processing File 5: Targeted extraction of YPLL and Poor Health metrics clean21_rankedmeasures <- read.csv("data/raw/2021/Ranked Measure Data.csv", skip = 1) %>% dplyr::select(FIPS, YPLL_Rate = contains("Potential.Life.Lost.Rate"), YPLL_Black = contains("YPLL.Rate..Black"), YPLL_White = contains("YPLL.Rate..White"), Percent_Poor_Health = contains("Fair.or.Poor.Health")) %>% filter(!is.na(YPLL_Rate)) 

```

# Relational Integration and Data Type Audit

The last step of the 2021 method was a multi-stage Relational Join using the Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) codes as the main key. After the join, I did a Data Type Audit. Because raw CSVs often import numeric rankings as character strings due to alphanumeric placeholders like "NR" (Not Reported), I converted these to numeric doubles.

# Synthesizing the 2021 Master Dataset

```{r join_and_audit_2021}

master_2021 <- clean21_measures %>% inner_join(clean21_ranked, by = "FIPS") %>% inner_join(clean21_subranked, by = "FIPS") %>% inner_join(clean21_rankedmeasures, by = "FIPS") # Auditing and forcing numeric types for mathematical validity master_2021$Median.Household.Income <- as.numeric(as.character(master_2021$Median.Household.Income)) master_2021$Health_Factors_Rank <- as.numeric(as.character(master_2021$Health_Factors_Rank)) master_2021$YPLL_Black <- as.numeric(as.character(master_2021$YPLL_Black)) master_2021$YPLL_White <- as.numeric(as.character(master_2021$YPLL_White)) # Listwise deletion of incomplete records master_2021 <- na.omit(master_2021) # Final Verification print(paste("Final 2021 County Count:", nrow(master_2021))) str(master_2021[c("Median.Household.Income", "YPLL_Black")])
```

---
title: "CSC 308 Final Project - Individual Contribution"
author: "Emmanuella Obidike"
date: "April 2026"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

# Load dataset
master_2021 <- read.csv("Master_2021_Cleaned.csv")
data21 <- master_2021
```

# Project Overview

This document represents my individual contribution to the group project titled Exploring Healthcare Access Disparities Through Data-Driven Visualization and Analysis. My work focuses on exploratory data analysis (EDA) and interpretation using the cleaned 2021 dataset.

The goal of this analysis is to examine relationships between income, healthcare access, and health outcomes across U.S. counties. By analyzing these patterns, we can better understand how socioeconomic conditions influence healthcare quality and overall population health.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Individual Contribution - Emmanuella Obidike

**Role:** Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), Statistical Analysis, and Interpretation

For this project, I analyzed the cleaned 2021 dataset to identify patterns between income levels, clinical care access, and health outcomes. My work included generating summary statistics, creating visualizations, performing correlation analysis, and interpreting key findings.

All code and analysis presented in this document reflect my individual contribution.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Dataset Description

The dataset used for this analysis is Master_2021_Cleaned.csv, which contains county-level data across the United States, including:

-   Median household income
-   Clinical care rankings
-   Health factors and health outcomes rankings
-   Demographic and health-related indicators

The analysis uses the full dataset (all U.S. counties) to provide a nationwide perspective on healthcare disparities.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Summary Statistics

```{r}
summary_table <- data21 %>%
  summarise(
    avg_income = mean(Median.Household.Income, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_clinical_rank = mean(Clinical_Care_Rank, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

summary_table
```

This summary provides an overall average of income and clinical care rankings across U.S. counties.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Visualization 1: Income vs Clinical Care

```{r}
ggplot(data21, aes(x = Median.Household.Income, y = Clinical_Care_Rank)) +
  geom_point(color = "blue", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Income vs Clinical Care Across U.S. Counties (2021)",
    x = "Median Household Income",
    y = "Clinical Care Rank (Lower = Better)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation**

This plot shows a negative relationship between income and clinical care rankings. Counties with higher median household income tend to have better (lower) clinical care rankings, suggesting that economic resources play a role in access to healthcare services.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Correlation: Income vs Clinical Care

```{r}
correlation <- cor(
  data21$Median.Household.Income,
  data21$Clinical_Care_Rank,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

correlation
```

**Interpretation**

The correlation is negative, indicating that as income increases, clinical care rankings improve. This supports the idea that income is an important factor in healthcare access.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Visualization 2: Health Factors vs Health Outcomes

```{r}
ggplot(data21, aes(x = Health_Factors_Rank, y = Health_Outcomes_Rank)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "darkgreen") +
  labs(
    title = "Health Factors vs Health Outcomes Across U.S. Counties (2021)",
    x = "Health Factors Rank (Lower = Better)",
    y = "Health Outcomes Rank (Lower = Better)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation**

There is a strong positive relationship between health factors and health outcomes. Counties with worse health factors tend to also have worse health outcomes, showing how environmental and behavioral conditions directly impact population health.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Correlation: Health Factors vs Outcomes

```{r}
correlation2 <- cor(
  data21$Health_Factors_Rank,
  data21$Health_Outcomes_Rank,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

correlation2
```

**Interpretation**

The correlation is very strong, confirming that health factors are closely linked to health outcomes across counties.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Visualization 3: Income Distribution

```{r}
ggplot(data21, aes(x = Median.Household.Income)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "purple", bins = 30, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Median Household Income (U.S. Counties, 2021)",
    x = "Median Household Income",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation**

Most counties fall within a middle-income range, with fewer counties at higher income levels, indicating a right-skewed distribution.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Visualization 4: Racial Disparities (YPLL)

```{r}
racial_data <- data21 %>%
  select(YPLL_Black, YPLL_White) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "Group", values_to = "YPLL")

ggplot(racial_data, aes(x = Group, y = YPLL, fill = Group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Racial Disparities in Premature Mortality (YPLL)",
    x = "Population Group",
    y = "YPLL Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation**

The plot shows noticeable differences in premature mortality between groups, suggesting disparities in health outcomes across populations.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Visualization 5: Income vs Health Outcomes

```{r}
ggplot(data21, aes(x = Median.Household.Income, y = Health_Outcomes_Rank)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "darkred") +
  labs(
    title = "Income vs Health Outcomes Across U.S. Counties (2021)",
    x = "Median Household Income",
    y = "Health Outcomes Rank"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation**

Higher income is generally associated with better health outcomes, though there is still variability across counties.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Visualization 6: Clinical Care Distribution

```{r}
ggplot(data21, aes(x = Clinical_Care_Rank)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "blue", bins = 30, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Clinical Care Rankings",
    x = "Clinical Care Rank",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation**

Clinical care rankings are concentrated at lower values (better rankings), with fewer counties having poor rankings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Conclusion

The analysis reveals clear relationships between socioeconomic conditions and healthcare access.

Higher income levels are associated with better clinical care rankings, suggesting that financial resources influence access to healthcare services. Additionally, the strong relationship between health factors and health outcomes shows that environmental and behavioral conditions play a major role in overall population health.

These patterns highlight broader disparities in healthcare access across U.S. counties

Overall, this analysis demonstrates how exploratory data analysis can transform complex datasets into meaningful insights about public health disparities.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Final Statement

All code and analysis in this document reflect my individual contribution to the project.

# -------------------------------

# Chloe Washington

# Role: Implications of Results, Analytical Discussion, and Interpretation

# -------------------------------

This report supports the analytical discussion component of the group project. The goal is to examine what the project's findings mean in a real-world healthcare context — specifically, how income disparities across U.S. counties relate to clinical care quality, and how behavioral and environmental health factors connect to population health outcomes.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Setup

Load the required libraries for data manipulation and visualization.

```{r libraries}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 1: Load the Dataset (2021)

We begin by loading the cleaned 2021 dataset created earlier in the project pipeline.

```{r load-data}
# Use the cleaned dataset created earlier in the project
data21 <- master_2021

# Quick check that the data loaded correctly
head(data21)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 2: Use Full Dataset (All U.S. Counties)

No state-level filtering is applied here. The analysis reflects all U.S. counties to ensure the findings are nationally representative.

```{r full-dataset}
# No filtering applied — analysis reflects all states
filtered_data <- data21
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 3: Summary Table by State

The table below shows the average median household income and average clinical care rank for each state. This provides a high-level view of regional variation in both economic conditions and healthcare quality.

```{r summary-table}
summary_table <- filtered_data %>%
  group_by(State) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_income        = mean(Median.Household.Income, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_clinical_rank = mean(Clinical_Care_Rank, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

summary_table
```

> **Interpretation:** Differences in average income across states highlight regional disparities that may contribute to unequal healthcare access and outcomes. States with lower average incomes tend to cluster toward worse clinical care rankings, suggesting a systemic relationship between economic resources and healthcare quality.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 4: Income vs. Clinical Care Rank (Scatter Plot)

The scatter plot below visualizes the relationship between median household income and clinical care rank at the county level across the United States in 2021.

```{r scatter-plot}
ggplot(filtered_data, aes(x = Median.Household.Income, y = Clinical_Care_Rank)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "#2C7BB6") +
  labs(
    title    = "Income vs. Clinical Care Across U.S. Counties (2021)",
    subtitle = "Each point represents one U.S. county",
    x        = "Median Household Income",
    y        = "Clinical Care Rank (Lower = Better)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

> **Interpretation:** The scatter plot reveals a downward trend — as median household income increases, clinical care rank tends to decrease (i.e., improve). This pattern points to a meaningful association between a county's economic standing and the quality of clinical care available to its residents.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 5: Correlation — Income and Clinical Care

To quantify the relationship observed in the scatter plot, we compute the Pearson correlation coefficient between median household income and clinical care rank.

```{r correlation-income-care}
correlation <- cor(
  filtered_data$Median.Household.Income,
  filtered_data$Clinical_Care_Rank,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cat("Correlation between income and clinical care rank:", round(correlation, 4))
```

> **Interpretation:** The negative correlation confirms that counties with higher incomes tend to have better (lower-numbered) clinical care rankings. This suggests that economic resources may play a significant role in shaping access to and quality of healthcare services. Counties with fewer financial resources may face systemic barriers — such as fewer healthcare providers, reduced insurance coverage, and limited infrastructure — that translate directly into worse care outcomes.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 6: Average Income by State (Bar Chart)

The bar chart below ranks all U.S. states by their average county-level median household income, providing a geographic view of economic inequality.

```{r bar-chart-income}
ggplot(summary_table, aes(x = reorder(State, avg_income), y = avg_income)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4DAF4A") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Average Median Household Income by State (2021)",
    subtitle = "Based on county-level averages",
    x        = "State",
    y        = "Average Income ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

> **Interpretation:** The bar chart makes visible the substantial income gap between states. States at the top of the chart — with higher average incomes — are generally located in the Northeast and West Coast, while lower-income states are concentrated in the South and rural Midwest. These geographic patterns mirror known disparities in healthcare infrastructure and outcomes, reinforcing the implication that place of residence is a strong predictor of healthcare access.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 7: Health Factors vs. Health Outcomes

Finally, we examine whether a county's overall health factors (behavioral, environmental, and social determinants) are associated with its health outcomes (length and quality of life).

```{r correlation-health}
correlation2 <- cor(
  filtered_data$Health_Factors_Rank,
  filtered_data$Health_Outcomes_Rank,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

cat("Correlation between health factors and health outcomes rank:", round(correlation2, 4))
```

> **Interpretation:** The strong positive correlation between health factors and health outcomes ranks indicates that counties with poorer underlying health conditions — such as high rates of smoking, obesity, or physical inactivity, combined with limited social and economic resources — also tend to experience worse overall health outcomes. This finding underscores the importance of upstream, preventive interventions that address root causes of poor health rather than focusing solely on clinical treatment after illness occurs.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conclusion

The analyses presented in this report consistently point to a central theme: **economic and environmental conditions are deeply intertwined with healthcare access and population health**. Key takeaways include:

-   Counties and states with higher median household incomes tend to rank better in clinical care quality.
-   Geographic disparities in income map closely onto disparities in healthcare access, suggesting that place of birth and residence remains a major determinant of health equity.
-   Health factors — encompassing behavioral, environmental, and socioeconomic influences — are strongly correlated with health outcomes, reinforcing the case for holistic, community-level health policy interventions.

These findings have real policy implications: improving healthcare access in underserved areas may require addressing income inequality, expanding insurance coverage, investing in rural and low-income healthcare infrastructure, and targeting public health programs at the communities with the greatest need.

---
title: "CSC 308 Final Project - Individual Portfolio"
author: "Joshua"
date: "April 2026"
output: html_document
---

This visualization explores the relationship between income and healthcare access. The results will show that lower income levels are associated with higher uninsured rates which portray that economic factors play a vital role in healthcare accessibility.

This analysis supports the overall findings of the project because it shows how financial disparities influence health outcomes across different populations.

---
title: "SidneyCerami_Validation_Markdown"
author: "Sidney Cerami"
date: "2026-04-20"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Individual Contribution - Sidney Cerami

**Role:** Validation, Data Quality, and Ethics Analysis

This section focuses on evaluating the reliability and quality of the dataset used in this project. The goal is to make sure the data is accurate, consistent, and appropriate for analysis while also considering ethical concerns.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Load Dataset

```{r}
master_2021 <- read.csv("processed_data/2021/Master_2021_Cleaned.csv")
dim(master_2021)
```

# Missing Values Check

```{r}
colSums(is.na(master_2021))
sum(is.na(master_2021))
```

This step checks for missing data that could affect results or reduce representation of certain counties.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Duplicate Check

```{r}
sum(duplicated(master_2021))
```

Ensures there are no repeated rows that could bias the analysis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Data Consistency

```{r}
invalid_income <- master_2021 %>%
  filter(Median.Household.Income < 0)

nrow(invalid_income)
summary(master_2021$Median.Household.Income)
```

This verifies that key variables like income contain valid values.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Summary of Key Variables

```{r}
summary(master_2021 %>%
          select(Median.Household.Income, Life.Expectancy))
```

Provides a general overview of how important variables are distributed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Data Reliability

The dataset comes from County Health Rankings, which uses sources like the CDC and U.S. Census Bureau. This makes the data generally reliable for analysis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Limitations and Bias

-   Data only covers one year (2021)
-   Missing values may affect some counties
-   Rural areas may be underrepresented
-   Reporting differences across regions may introduce bias

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Ethical Considerations

-   Avoid misrepresenting groups
-   Do not generalize results to individuals
-   Be transparent about limitations

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Conclusion

The dataset is suitable for analysis, but it has some limitations and potential biases. These factors should be considered when interpreting the results.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Final Statement

All validation, data quality checks, and ethical analysis in this document reflect my individual contribution to the project.
